title: From Desacralization To Resacralization Of Power
gist: Didier Darcet argues that the historical arc of Western politics — stripping power of its sacred legitimacy to ground it in popular sovereignty — may now be reversing. Contemporary capitalism, he suggests, is re-enchanting power with a quasi-sacred aura, this time through economic rather than religious form.

insights.claim: The centuries-long political project of desacralizing power — separating rule from divine mandate — may be going into reverse, with economic power now acquiring a sacred character analogous to that once held by monarchs and emperors.
insights.speaker: Didier Darcet
insights.so_what: If economic elites are accruing legitimacy that was once reserved for divinely ordained rulers, it implies increasing tolerance for concentrated wealth and power, with implications for regulatory risk, tax policy, and social stability — all material to equity and political-risk premia.
insights.quote: contemporary capitalism may be turning this trajectory on its head. After centuries of desacralization, recent decades suggest power may be acquiring a sacred character again, this time in economic form.

insights.claim: For millennia political authority derived its legitimacy from the sacred — kings via heavenly mandate, emperors as intermediaries between men and gods — before modern democracies relocated legitimacy to popular sovereignty.
insights.speaker: Didier Darcet
insights.so_what: Establishes the historical baseline: the secular-democratic consensus that has underpinned liberal market economies since the Enlightenment is the anomaly, not the norm — making its erosion structurally significant rather than cyclical noise.
insights.quote: For millennia, to rule was to embody the divine: kings governed by heavenly mandate, emperors stood as intermediaries between men and gods, authority drew its legitimacy from the sacred.

insights.claim: The shift from sacred to secular power culminated in the rise of modern democracies, where authority rose from the people rather than descending from heaven — a process the author frames as the completed arc of desacralization.
insights.speaker: Didier Darcet
insights.so_what: Framing democracy as the end-point of a multi-millennium desacralization process contextualizes current democratic backsliding as a potential phase transition, not mere populist volatility — relevant for sovereign risk and long-duration asset pricing.
insights.quote: power slowly separated itself from the sacred, culminating in the rise of modern democracies, where authority no longer descended from heaven but rose from the people.

insights.claim: The article positions recent decades — not a specific political event — as the inflection point at which economic power began re-acquiring sacred qualities, suggesting the trend is already underway rather than hypothetical.
insights.speaker: Didier Darcet
insights.so_what: The multi-decade framing suggests this is a structural regime shift rather than a cyclical phenomenon, arguing against mean-reversion trades premised on a return to mid-20th-century democratic norms.
insights.quote: recent decades suggest power may be acquiring a sacred character again, this time in economic form.

insights.claim: The article is framed as a Gavekal-IS (Investment Strategy) piece, signaling the author intends to derive actionable investment implications from what is presented as a structural political-economy shift.
insights.speaker: Didier Darcet
insights.so_what: The IS label indicates the full article likely connects this macro-political thesis to specific positioning ideas — worth accessing for investors tracking political-risk overlays.
